2026-W17.title: Context Unrolling in Omni Models
2026-W17.description: Trains a unified multimodal model natively on text, images, videos, 3D geometry, and hidden representations to enable cross-modal reasoning and generation.
2026-W17.contribution: Introduces 'Context Unrolling', a mechanism where a natively multimodal model explicitly reasons across multiple modal representations before producing predictions, allowing it to aggregate complementary information from heterogeneous modalities. This approach facilitates a more faithful approximation of a shared multimodal knowledge manifold, distinguishing it from prior work that typically handles modalities more independently or sequentially.
2026-W17.limitations: The paper assumes that joint training across diverse modalities inherently yields a shared knowledge manifold, and it is unclear how the model scales or generalizes to modalities not seen during training.
2026-W17.filter_reason: Proposes a unified multimodal architecture with native support for 5+ modalities and introduces 'Context Unrolling' as a novel mechanism for cross-modal reasoning that demonstrates qualitatively new capabilities in multimodal generation and understanding.
2026-W17.rating_rationale: ByteDance AI is tier-1 industry lab (pedigree=3). Context Unrolling is a meaningful new mechanism for cross-modal reasoning that passed novelty filter but doesn't constitute a paradigm shift (novelty=2). Unified multimodal architecture advances the multimodal/video generation subfield substantially (breadth=2, topic=3). No code/demo mentioned yet (code=1). Among early papers on unified omni-models with this mechanism (timing=2). Likely moderate hype from flashy multimodal capabilities (hype=2). Total = 0.30×1.0 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0.0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×1.0 = 0.70 → 3 stars.

2026-W17.title: Seeing Fast and Slow: Learning the Flow of Time in Videos
2026-W17.description: Develops models that learn to perceive, reason about, and manipulate the flow of time in videos, enabling speed detection, estimation, and temporal control in generation.
2026-W17.contribution: The paper introduces a self-supervised framework for learning temporal reasoning from multimodal cues in videos, treating time as a learnable visual concept rather than a fixed axis. This enables automatic curation of a large-scale slow-motion video dataset from noisy in-the-wild sources, overcoming the scarcity of high-speed camera footage. Building on this, the work presents speed-conditioned video generation and temporal super-resolution as novel capabilities grounded in learned temporal understanding.
2026-W17.limitations: The approach relies on self-supervised signals naturally present in videos, which may be noisy or inconsistent across different video sources. The curation pipeline for slow-motion data depends on the quality of the learned speed estimation models, potentially propagating errors into downstream training.
2026-W17.filter_reason: The paper frames temporal dynamics as a learnable visual concept with self-supervised learning for speed detection, enabling new capabilities in speed-conditioned video generation and temporal super-resolution—qualitatively new competencies for controllable video generation that are relevant to frontier video generation research.
2026-W17.rating_rationale: Strong fit for primary interest (video generation with novel speed-conditioning capability), solid university pedigree (UW is tier-1), meaningful new capability that passed novelty filter but not paradigm-shifting, moderate breadth within video generation subfield, no code/demo mentioned yet, early but not first-mover on temporal control in video generation.

2026-W17.title: Vista4D: Video Reshooting with 4D Point Clouds
2026-W17.description: Reshoots input videos from new camera trajectories by grounding scene content in a 4D point cloud representation.
2026-W17.contribution: Vista4D introduces a 4D-grounded point cloud representation that combines static pixel segmentation with 4D reconstruction to explicitly preserve scene appearance and provide rich camera control signals. Unlike prior methods that struggle with depth estimation artifacts in dynamic scenes, it trains on reconstructed multiview dynamic data to achieve robustness against point cloud artifacts at inference time, enabling precise control over challenging novel camera trajectories.
2026-W17.limitations: The method relies on 4D reconstruction and static pixel segmentation, which may fail under severe occlusions or highly complex dynamic scenes where reconstruction quality is inherently limited. Performance is also dependent on the quality of the underlying point cloud, which can degrade for real-world videos with extreme motion or texture-less regions.
2026-W17.filter_reason: Proposes a novel 4D point cloud grounding approach for video reshooting that addresses fundamental challenges in dynamic scene re-synthesis with explicit mechanisms for content preservation and camera control, demonstrating qualitatively new capabilities in video generation.
2026-W17.rating_rationale: Vista4D addresses video generation with novel 4D point cloud grounding for dynamic scene re-synthesis—a meaningful contribution directly aligned with primary interest in world models and video generation. Mixed-tier affiliations (Netflix, Columbia, UCLA, Oxford) with no tier-1 lab backing limit pedigree; passed novelty filter but not paradigm-shifting; moderate breadth in video gen; good topic fit (3) but limited code/demo release info and modest social-media potential yield 2 stars.

2026-W17.title: Quotient-Space Diffusion Models
2026-W17.description: Establishes a formal diffusion modeling framework on quotient spaces to handle symmetry groups in generative tasks such as molecular structure generation.
2026-W17.contribution: The key insight is that by defining diffusion directly on the quotient space (e.g., modding out SE(3) symmetry), the model avoids learning the redundant group-action component, reducing learning difficulty compared to group-equivariant diffusion models. Unlike heuristic alignment strategies, this framework provides a principled sampler that is guaranteed to recover the target distribution. This offers a unified theoretical foundation for symmetry-aware generative modeling that goes beyond ad hoc equivariance constraints.
2026-W17.limitations: The framework is primarily validated on SE(3) symmetry for molecular generation, and its generalizability to other symmetry groups or more complex quotient spaces is not fully explored. The practical construction of diffusion processes and samplers on general quotient spaces may be non-trivial and problem-specific.
2026-W17.filter_reason: Proposes a novel theoretical framework for diffusion modeling on quotient spaces that formally handles symmetries in generative tasks, reducing learning complexity compared to existing group-equivariant approaches with principled guarantees.
affiliations: State Key Laboratory of General Artificial Intelligence, Peking University
State Key Laboratory of Human-Machine Hybrid Augmented Intelligence, Institute of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics, Xi'an Jiaotong University
Huazhong University of Science and Technology
2026-W17.rating_rationale: Microsoft Research Asia affiliation triggers pedigree=3 per rules; quotient-space framework is a meaningful theoretical contribution (novelty=2, passes filter but not paradigm-shift); advances diffusion modeling with symmetry handling—applicable to generation tasks broadly (breadth=2); lacks social-media appeal and code/demo visibility (hype=1, code=1); among early works on this theoretical angle (timing=2); diffusion relevance to video/image generation places it at secondary-interest level (topic=2). Total: 0.30×1.0 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 = 0.575 → 2 stars.

2026-W17.title: Reshoot-Anything: A Self-Supervised Model for In-the-Wild Video Reshooting
2026-W17.description: Trains a self-supervised diffusion transformer to reshoot dynamic videos with precise camera control by learning from internet-scale monocular videos without paired multi-view data.
2026-W17.contribution: The key novelty is a scalable self-supervised framework that generates pseudo multi-view training triplets from single monocular videos using independent random-walk crop trajectories as source/target pairs and a synthetically forward-warped geometric anchor. The independent cropping strategy forces the model to implicitly learn 4D spatiotemporal structures by routing and re-projecting textures across time and viewpoints rather than simply copying from the current frame, enabling generalization to real dynamic scenes at inference using a 4D point-cloud anchor.
2026-W17.limitations: The approach relies on synthetic pseudo multi-view data generated from monocular videos, which may introduce domain gaps compared to real multi-view captures. The geometric anchor is derived from dense tracking and forward-warping of a single frame, which may fail for scenes with extreme motion or large unobserved regions.
2026-W17.filter_reason: Proposes a novel self-supervised training framework that generates pseudo multi-view data from monocular video through geometric anchors and spatial misalignment, enabling a qualitatively new capability for temporal video reshooting with precise camera control at scale.
2026-W17.rating_rationale: Texas A&M is a respected university (score 2), self-supervised multi-view generation from monocular video is a meaningful capability that advances video generation (novelty and breadth both 2), video reshooting has moderate hype potential with visual demos, no code/demo release mentioned yet, and this directly targets primary interest area (video generation with spatial/temporal control), yielding total = 0.30×0.5 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×1.0 = 0.55, borderline 2 stars.

2026-W18.title: GLM-5V-Turbo: Toward a Native Foundation Model for Multimodal Agents
2026-W18.abstract: We present GLM-5V-Turbo, a step toward native foundation models for multimodal agents. As foundation models are increasingly deployed in real environments, agentic capability depends not only on language reasoning, but also on the ability to perceive, interpret, and act over heterogeneous contexts such as images, videos, webpages, documents, GUIs. GLM-5V-Turbo is built around this objective: multimodal perception is integrated as a core component of reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution, rather than as an auxiliary interface to a language model. This report summarizes the main improvements behind GLM-5V-Turbo across model design, multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and integration with agent frameworks. These developments lead to strong performance in multimodal coding, visual tool use, and framework-based agentic tasks, while preserving competitive text-only coding capability. More importantly, our development process offers practical insights for building multimodal agents, highlighting the central role of multimodal perception, hierarchical optimization, and reliable end-to-end verification.
2026-W18.filter_reason: This paper presents a native multimodal foundation model that integrates perception as a core reasoning component rather than auxiliary, with systematic improvements across model design, multimodal training, and RL—demonstrating qualitatively new agentic capabilities across heterogeneous contexts (images, videos, webpages, GUIs).
2026-W18.rating_rationale: GLM-5V-Turbo from Tsinghua/ByteDance (tier-1 lab) advances multimodal agent capabilities with native integration of perception into reasoning—a meaningful step for your world models/video/multimodal focus—but represents engineering-driven improvement (novelty=2) rather than paradigm-shift, with no released code yet; total score ≈0.70 = 3 stars.
2026-W18.description: Integrates multimodal perception as a core component of reasoning, planning, tool use, and execution in a foundation model designed natively for agentic tasks.
2026-W18.contribution: Rather than treating vision as an auxiliary interface bolted onto a language model, GLM-5V-Turbo treats multimodal perception as a first-class citizen in the model architecture and training pipeline. The work combines multimodal training, reinforcement learning, toolchain expansion, and agent framework integration to support heterogeneous contexts such as images, videos, webpages, documents, and GUIs. It also offers practical insights around hierarchical optimization and reliable end-to-end verification for building multimodal agents.
2026-W18.limitations: The paper is primarily a technical report describing engineering and design choices rather than proposing a rigorously evaluated novel algorithm, so claims about architectural novelty are not formally ablated. The approach is evaluated within specific agentic frameworks, which may limit generalizability to other deployment environments.

2026-W18.title: 3D-ReGen: A Unified 3D Geometry Regeneration Framework
2026-W18.abstract: We consider the problem of regenerating 3D objects from 2D images and initial 3D shapes. Most 3D generators operate in a one-shot fashion, converting text or images to a 3D object with limited controllability. We introduce instead 3D-ReGen, a 3D regenerator that is conditioned on an initial 3D shape. This conceptually simple formulation allows us to support numerous useful tasks, including 3D enhancement, reconstruction, and editing. 3D-ReGen uses a new conditioning mechanism based on VecSet, which allows the regenerator to update or improve the input geometry with consistent fine-grained details. 3D-ReGen learns a widely applicable regeneration prior from off-the-shelf 3D datasets via self-supervised pretext tasks and augmentations, without additional annotations. We evaluate both the geometric consistency and fine-grained quality of 3D-ReGen, achieving state-of-the-art performance in controllable 3D generation across several tasks.
2026-W18.filter_reason: Proposes a novel conditioning mechanism (VecSet-based) for 3D generation that enables a unified framework supporting multiple tasks (enhancement, reconstruction, editing) with a new self-supervised pretext task formulation, representing a meaningful architectural and methodological contribution beyond standard technique application.
2026-W18.rating_rationale: Facebook AI Research (Meta AI Research) is a tier-1 lab triggering mandatory pedigree=3. Novel conditioning mechanism and self-supervised formulation warrant novelty=2 (passed filter but not paradigm-shifting). 3D generation advances one subfield with meaningful but not foundational impact (breadth=2). Topic is 3D/geometry-focused rather than world models or video generation (topic=2). No code/demo mentioned (code=1). Among early work on unified 3D conditioning (timing=2). Social interest moderate for broader ML community (hype=2). Total: 0.30×1.0 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 = 0.625 → 2 stars.
2026-W18.description: Introduces a unified 3D regeneration framework that refines and edits 3D objects conditioned on both 2D images and initial 3D shapes.
2026-W18.contribution: Unlike one-shot 3D generators that lack controllability, 3D-ReGen conditions generation on an initial 3D shape using a novel VecSet-based conditioning mechanism, enabling fine-grained geometry updates with consistency. The framework learns a generalizable regeneration prior through self-supervised pretext tasks and augmentations from existing 3D datasets without requiring additional annotations, supporting diverse tasks like enhancement, reconstruction, and editing within a single model.
2026-W18.limitations: The approach relies on the quality and diversity of off-the-shelf 3D datasets for learning its prior, which may limit generalization to object categories or geometric configurations underrepresented in those datasets. The method assumes an initial 3D shape is available as input, making it dependent on an upstream shape source rather than operating purely from 2D observations.

2026-W18.title: Mutual Forcing: Dual-Mode Self-Evolution for Fast Autoregressive Audio-Video Character Generation
2026-W18.abstract: In this work, we propose Mutual Forcing, a framework for fast autoregressive audio-video generation with long-horizon audio-video synchronization. Our approach addresses two key challenges: joint audio-video modeling and fast autoregressive generation. To ease joint audio-video optimization, we adopt a two-stage training strategy: we first train uni-modal generators and then couple them into a unified audio-video model for joint training on paired data. For streaming generation, we ask whether a native fast causal audio-video model can be trained directly, instead of following existing streaming distillation pipelines that typically train a bidirectional model first and then convert it into a causal generator through multiple distillation stages. Our answer is Mutual Forcing, which builds directly on native autoregressive model and integrates few-step and multi-step generation within a single weight-shared model, enabling self-distillation and improved training-inference consistency. The multi-step mode improves the few-step mode via self-distillation, while the few-step mode generates historical context during training to improve training-inference consistency; because the two modes share parameters, these two effects reinforce each other within a single model. Compared with prior approaches such as Self-Forcing, Mutual Forcing removes the need for an additional bidirectional teacher model, supports more flexible training sequence lengths, reduces training overhead, and allows the model to improve directly from real paired data rather than a fixed teacher. Experiments show that Mutual Forcing matches or surpasses strong baselines that require around 50 sampling steps while using only 4 to 8 steps, demonstrating substantial advantages in both efficiency and quality. The project page is available at https://mutualforcing.github.io.
affiliations: VCIP, School of Computer Science, Nankai University
2026-W18.filter_reason: The paper proposes Mutual Forcing, a novel training framework that enables native fast autoregressive audio-video generation through dual-mode self-distillation without requiring separate bidirectional teachers, achieving 6-12x speedup—a meaningful architectural and methodological contribution to efficient multimodal generation.
2026-W18.rating_rationale: Strong fit for primary interest (video generation + multimodal); authors from respected Chinese institutions with solid ML track record but not tier-1 lab affiliations; Mutual Forcing is a meaningful methodological advance in efficient autoregressive generation (passing novelty filter but not paradigm-shifting); project page and demo mentioned; achieves 6-12× speedup, enabling practical fast generation—solid 2-star paper with excellent topic alignment.
2026-W18.description: Introduces Mutual Forcing, a framework that trains a native fast causal autoregressive model for joint audio-video character generation by integrating few-step and multi-step generation within a single weight-shared model.
2026-W18.contribution: Mutual Forcing eliminates the need for a separate bidirectional teacher model by enabling self-distillation directly within a single weight-shared model that operates in both few-step and multi-step modes. The few-step mode improves training-inference consistency by generating historical context during training, while the multi-step mode improves the few-step mode via self-distillation, creating a mutually reinforcing cycle within one model. This approach supports flexible training sequence lengths, reduces training overhead, and allows the model to learn directly from real paired data rather than a fixed teacher.
2026-W18.limitations: The approach relies on a two-stage training strategy that first requires uni-modal pre-training before joint audio-video coupling, which may constrain how deeply the modalities are co-learned from the start. The self-distillation mechanism depends on the quality of the model's own multi-step outputs, which could propagate errors if the base model is insufficiently trained.

2026-W18.title: AnimateAnyMesh++: A Flexible 4D Foundation Model for High-Fidelity Text-Driven Mesh Animation
2026-W18.abstract: Recent advances in 4D content generation have attracted increasing attention, yet creating high-quality animated 3D models remains challenging due to the complexity of modeling spatio-temporal distributions and the scarcity of 4D training data. We present AnimateAnyMesh++, a feed-forward framework for text-driven animation of arbitrary 3D meshes with substantial upgrades in data, architecture, and generative capability. First, we expand the DyMesh-XL dataset by mining dynamic content from Objaverse-XL, increasing the number of unique identities from 60K to 300K and substantially broadening category and motion diversity. Second, we redesign DyMeshVAE-Flex with power-law topology-aware attention and vertex-normal enhanced features, which significantly improves trajectory reconstruction, local geometry preservation, and mitigates trajectory-sticking artifacts. Third, we introduce architectural changes to both DyMeshVAE-Flex and the rectified-flow (RF) generator to support variable-length sequence training and generation, enabling longer animations while preserving reconstruction fidelity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnimateAnyMesh++ generates semantically accurate and temporally coherent mesh animations within seconds, surpassing prior approaches in quality and efficiency. The enlarged DyMesh-XL, the upgraded DyMeshVAE-Flex, and variable-length RF together deliver consistent gains across benchmarks and in-the-wild meshes. We will release code, models, and the expanded DyMesh-XL upon acceptance of this manuscript to facilitate research in 4D content creation.
2026-W18.filter_reason: The paper introduces several novel architectural components (power-law topology-aware attention, vertex-normal enhanced features, variable-length sequence training for rectified flow) that enable a qualitatively new capability in 4D generation—high-fidelity text-driven mesh animation with longer sequences—supported by a substantially expanded dataset.
2026-W18.rating_rationale: AnimateAnyMesh++ makes solid incremental advances (enlarged dataset, improved VAE architecture, variable-length generation) in 4D mesh animation with promising results and stated code release, authored by active researchers at a respected institution (Huazhong University of Science and Technology); it directly targets world models/video generation (primary interest, topic=3) but lacks the paradigm-shift novelty or author prestige of top-tier labs needed for higher impact.
2026-W18.description: Introduces a feed-forward framework for text-driven animation of arbitrary 3D meshes using an improved VAE and rectified-flow generator to produce temporally coherent 4D content.
2026-W18.contribution: The work introduces three key innovations: a topology-aware attention mechanism with power-law weighting and vertex-normal enhanced features in DyMeshVAE-Flex that improves local geometry preservation and eliminates trajectory-sticking artifacts; variable-length sequence support in both the VAE and rectified-flow generator enabling longer animations; and a massively expanded 4D training dataset (DyMesh-XL) scaled from 60K to 300K unique identities mined from Objaverse-XL, broadening category and motion diversity for 4D generation.
2026-W18.limitations: The approach depends on the availability and quality of dynamic mesh data mined from Objaverse-XL, which may still underrepresent certain motion types or object categories. The method is a feed-forward model that may struggle with highly complex or physically precise motions not well-covered by the training distribution.

2026-W18.title: Generate Your Talking Avatar from Video Reference
2026-W18.abstract: Existing talking avatar methods typically adopt an image-to-video pipeline conditioned on a static reference image within the same scene as the target generation. This restricted, single-view perspective lacks sufficient temporal and expression cues, limiting the ability to synthesize high-fidelity talking avatars in customized backgrounds. To this end, we introduce Talking Avatar generation from Video Reference (TAVR), a novel framework that shifts the paradigm by leveraging cross-scene video inputs. To effectively process these extended temporal contexts and bridge cross-scene domain gaps, TAVR integrates a token selection module alongside a comprehensive three-stage training scheme. Specifically, same-scene video pretraining establishes foundational appearance copying, which is subsequently expanded by cross-scene reference fine-tuning for robust cross-scene adaptation. Finally, task-specific reinforcement learning aligns the generated outputs with identity-based rewards to maximize identity similarity. To systematically evaluate cross-scene robustness, we construct a new benchmark comprising 158 carefully curated cross-scene video pairs. Extensive experiments show that TAVR benefits from flexible inference-time video referencing and consistently surpasses existing baselines both quantitatively and qualitatively. This work has been deployed to production. For more related research, please visit \href{https://www.heygen.com/research}{HeyGen Research} and \href{https://www.heygen.com/research/avatar-v-model}{HeyGen Avatar-V}.
2026-W18.filter_reason: The paper introduces a novel paradigm shift from single-image to cross-scene video reference for talking avatars, with a three-stage training scheme (same-scene pretraining, cross-scene fine-tuning, RL alignment) and token selection module that addresses the non-trivial challenge of bridging domain gaps across scenes, plus a new benchmark for systematic evaluation.
2026-W18.rating_rationale: HeyGen is a respected industry player in avatar generation with production deployment, the cross-scene video reference paradigm is a meaningful advance (not a fundamental breakthrough), the work is clearly relevant to video generation (primary interest) with moderate breadth, and the production status + new benchmark suggest solid engineering and visibility, yielding a borderline 2–3 star assessment.
2026-W18.description: Introduces a framework that generates talking avatars by leveraging cross-scene video references rather than static single-scene images.
2026-W18.contribution: TAVR shifts the talking avatar paradigm from image-conditioned, single-scene generation to cross-scene video-conditioned generation, enabling richer temporal and expression cues across different backgrounds. It combines a token selection module with a three-stage training scheme—same-scene pretraining, cross-scene fine-tuning, and task-specific reinforcement learning with identity-based rewards—to bridge domain gaps and maximize identity similarity. The use of RL with identity rewards to align generated outputs is a novel training signal for this task.
2026-W18.limitations: The method relies on a three-stage training pipeline that may be complex to reproduce or extend, and its evaluation is limited to a benchmark of 158 cross-scene video pairs which may not cover all real-world diversity.

2026-W20.title: Normalizing Trajectory Models
2026-W20.abstract: Diffusion-based models decompose sampling into many small Gaussian denoising steps -- an assumption that breaks down when generation is compressed to a few coarse transitions. Existing few-step methods address this through distillation, consistency training, or adversarial objectives, but sacrifice the likelihood framework in the process. We introduce Normalizing Trajectory Models (NTM), which models each reverse step as an expressive conditional normalizing flow with exact likelihood training. Architecturally, NTM combines shallow invertible blocks within each step with a deep parallel predictor across the trajectory, forming an end-to-end network trainable from scratch or initializable from pretrained flow-matching models. Its exact trajectory likelihood further enables self-distillation: a lightweight denoiser trained on the model's own score produces high-quality samples in four steps. On text-to-image benchmarks, NTM matches or outperforms strong image generation baselines in just four sampling steps while uniquely retaining exact likelihood over the generative trajectory.
2026-W20.filter_reason: Proposes a novel architectural combination (normalizing flows as reverse steps) with a new training objective (exact likelihood in few-step generation) that enables both fast sampling and tractable likelihood computation, addressing a fundamental limitation of diffusion models.
2026-W20.rating_rationale: Google affiliation triggers pedigree=3; normalizing flows for few-step generation is a meaningful contribution (novelty=2) with solid impact on image generation and potential multimodal applications (breadth=2, topic=2), but lacks the paradigm-shift quality for higher novelty, and no code/demo release mentioned limits practical impact.
2026-W20.description: Introduces Normalizing Trajectory Models (NTM), which models each reverse diffusion step as an expressive conditional normalizing flow enabling exact likelihood training for high-quality few-step image generation.
2026-W20.contribution: NTM uniquely combines shallow invertible blocks within each denoising step with a deep parallel predictor across the full trajectory, preserving exact likelihood throughout the generative process — something prior few-step methods (distillation, consistency training, adversarial objectives) sacrifice. This exact trajectory likelihood enables a self-distillation scheme where a lightweight denoiser trained on the model's own score produces high-quality samples in four steps without external teacher signals. The architecture can be trained from scratch or initialized from pretrained flow-matching models, bridging normalizing flows and diffusion-based generation.
2026-W20.limitations: The method relies on invertible architectures that may constrain representational capacity compared to unconstrained networks used in standard diffusion models. The approach is evaluated primarily in the few-step (four-step) regime, and it is unclear how it scales or compares in full many-step generation settings.

2026-W20.title: Qwen-Image-2.0 Technical Report
2026-W20.abstract: We present Qwen-Image-2.0, an omni-capable image generation foundation model that unifies high-fidelity generation and precise image editing within a single framework. Despite recent progress, existing models still struggle with ultra-long text rendering, multilingual typography, high-resolution photorealism, robust instruction following, and efficient deployment, especially in text-rich and compositionally complex scenarios. Qwen-Image-2.0 addresses these challenges by coupling Qwen3-VL as the condition encoder with a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer for joint condition-target modeling, supported by large-scale data curation and a customized multi-stage training pipeline. This enables strong multimodal understanding while preserving flexible generation and editing capabilities. The model supports instructions of up to 1K tokens for generating text-rich content such as slides, posters, infographics, and comics, while significantly improving multilingual text fidelity and typography. It also enhances photorealistic generation with richer details, more realistic textures, and coherent lighting, and follows complex prompts more reliably across diverse styles. Extensive human evaluations show that Qwen-Image-2.0 substantially outperforms previous Qwen-Image models in both generation and editing, marking a step toward more general, reliable, and practical image generation foundation models.
2026-W20.filter_reason: Demonstrates qualitatively new capabilities in image generation (ultra-long text rendering, multilingual typography, high-resolution photorealism) through a novel unified framework coupling vision-language understanding with diffusion transformers for both generation and editing at scale.
2026-W20.rating_rationale: Alibaba/Qwen is a tier-1 industry lab (ByteDance AI equivalent in prestige); unified generation+editing with text rendering is meaningful but incremental; moderate breadth in image gen ecosystem; likely moderate social attention; no code/demo confirmed; among first but not first-mover on multimodal diffusion transformers; primary interest is world models/video (lower relevance) though secondary interest in image generation applies. Total: 0.30×1.0 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 = 0.58 → 2 stars.
2026-W20.description: Unifies high-fidelity image generation and precise image editing within a single framework capable of handling ultra-long text rendering, multilingual typography, and complex compositional prompts.
2026-W20.contribution: Qwen-Image-2.0 couples Qwen3-VL as a condition encoder with a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer for joint condition-target modeling, enabling rich multimodal understanding alongside flexible generation and editing in one model. A key novelty is supporting instructions of up to 1K tokens to generate text-rich, compositionally complex content such as slides, posters, infographics, and comics with strong multilingual typography fidelity. This is further supported by large-scale data curation and a customized multi-stage training pipeline tailored for both generation and editing tasks.
2026-W20.limitations: The paper does not explicitly discuss failure cases or out-of-distribution scenarios, and the reliance on large-scale curated data and a heavy VL encoder (Qwen3-VL) may pose significant computational and deployment costs despite efficiency being cited as a goal.

2026-W20.title: Pixal3D: Pixel-Aligned 3D Generation from Images
2026-W20.abstract: Recent advances in 3D generative models have rapidly improved image-to-3D synthesis quality, enabling higher-resolution geometry and more realistic appearance. Yet fidelity, which measures pixel-level faithfulness of the generated 3D asset to the input image, still remains a central bottleneck. We argue this stems from an implicit 2D-3D correspondence issue: most 3D-native generators synthesize shape in canonical space and inject image cues via attention, leaving pixel-to-3D associations ambiguous. To tackle this issue, we draw inspiration from 3D reconstruction and propose Pixal3D, a pixel-aligned 3D generation paradigm for high-fidelity 3D asset creation from images. Instead of generating in a canonical pose, Pixal3D directly generates 3D in a pixel-aligned way, consistent with the input view. To enable this, we introduce a pixel back-projection conditioning scheme that explicitly lifts multi-scale image features into a 3D feature volume, establishing direct pixel-to-3D correspondence without ambiguity. We show that Pixal3D is not only scalable and capable of producing high-quality 3D assets, but also substantially improves fidelity, approaching the fidelity level of reconstruction. Furthermore, Pixal3D naturally extends to multi-view generation by aggregating back-projected feature volumes across views. Finally, we show pixel-aligned generation benefits scene synthesis, and present a modular pipeline that produces high-fidelity, object-separated 3D scenes from images. Pixal3D for the first time demonstrates 3D-native pixel-aligned generation at scale, and provides a new inspiring way towards high-fidelity 3D generation of object or scene from single or multi-view images. Project page: https://ldyang694.github.io/projects/pixal3d/
affiliations: BNRist, Department of Computer Science and Technology, Tsinghua University
2026-W20.filter_reason: Proposes a novel pixel-aligned 3D generation paradigm with explicit pixel back-projection conditioning that fundamentally addresses the 2D-3D correspondence problem in image-to-3D synthesis, enabling higher fidelity generation at scale.
2026-W20.rating_rationale: Pixel-aligned 3D generation addresses a real bottleneck (2D-3D correspondence) in image-to-3D synthesis with a well-motivated paradigm shift in approach, directly relevant to world models and 3D scene understanding; strong university affiliation and project page suggest solid execution, but authors are not prominent names and this is a meaningful advance rather than transformative breakthrough.
2026-W20.description: Introduces a pixel-aligned 3D generation paradigm that directly generates 3D assets consistent with the input view by explicitly lifting multi-scale image features into a 3D feature volume.
2026-W20.contribution: Unlike prior 3D-native generators that synthesize shape in canonical space and inject image cues via attention (leaving pixel-to-3D associations ambiguous), Pixal3D introduces a pixel back-projection conditioning scheme that establishes direct, unambiguous pixel-to-3D correspondence. This paradigm generates 3D content in a pixel-aligned manner consistent with the input view rather than in a canonical pose, bridging the gap between generation and reconstruction fidelity. The approach naturally extends to multi-view inputs by aggregating back-projected feature volumes across views and enables modular high-fidelity scene synthesis.
2026-W20.limitations: The method relies on accurate pixel back-projection, which may be sensitive to the quality of depth or camera pose estimates when handling in-the-wild images. The paper does not explicitly address failure cases for heavily occluded objects or scenes with complex geometry that lacks clear pixel-to-3D correspondence.

2026-W20.title: MoCoTalk: Multi-Conditional Diffusion with Adaptive Router for Controllable Talking Head Generation
2026-W20.abstract: Talking-head generation requires joint modeling of identity, head pose, facial expression, and mouth dynamics. Existing methods typically address only a subset of these factors, and rely on fixed-weight or heuristic fusion when multiple conditions are involved. We present MoCoTalk, a multi-conditional video diffusion framework that unifies four complementary control signals: a reference image, facial keypoints, 3DMM-rendered shading meshes, and the corresponding speech audio. To resolve destructive interference among heterogeneous conditions, we introduce an Adaptive Multi-Condition Router that computes channel-wise, timestep-aware gating over the four condition streams, allowing the fusion strategy to vary with both feature subspace and noise level. To better capture speech-related facial dynamics, we design a Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh, a 3DMM-based representation that decouples head motion, mouth motion, expression, and lighting. This design provides a temporally consistent geometric prior and allows flexible recombination of these attributes at inference. We further introduce a lip consistency loss to tighten audio-visual alignment. Extensive experiments show that MoCoTalk achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of structural, motion, and perceptual metrics, while offering attribute-level controllability that single-condition methods do not provide.
2026-W20.filter_reason: The paper introduces novel architectural components (Adaptive Multi-Condition Router with channel-wise timestep-aware gating, Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh) that address a non-trivial technical problem of destructive interference in multi-conditional diffusion, demonstrating architectural insight beyond standard technique application.
2026-W20.rating_rationale: MoCoTalk demonstrates solid technical contributions (adaptive routing, mouth-augmented mesh) to multi-conditional video diffusion with strong experimental results, directly aligned with primary research interests in video generation; however, the authors lack tier-1 industry lab affiliation or world-renowned names, the novelty is meaningful but not paradigm-shifting (incremental advances in existing diffusion frameworks), and no code/demo release is mentioned, yielding a mid-tier assessment of 2 stars.
2026-W20.description: Introduces a multi-conditional video diffusion framework that unifies four control signals—reference image, facial keypoints, 3DMM-rendered shading meshes, and speech audio—for controllable talking head generation.
2026-W20.contribution: The key novelty is an Adaptive Multi-Condition Router that computes channel-wise, timestep-aware gating over heterogeneous condition streams, allowing fusion strategy to adapt to both feature subspace and diffusion noise level rather than using fixed-weight or heuristic fusion. Additionally, the Mouth-Augmented Shading Mesh is a new 3DMM-based representation that explicitly decouples head motion, mouth motion, expression, and lighting into independently recombineable attributes. A lip consistency loss is further introduced to tighten audio-visual alignment during training.
2026-W20.limitations: The method relies on 3DMM fitting quality, which may introduce errors for extreme poses or occlusions. The framework's complexity from four simultaneous condition streams may increase computational cost compared to single-condition approaches.

2026-W20.title: What Matters for Diffusion-Friendly Latent Manifold? Prior-Aligned Autoencoders for Latent Diffusion
2026-W20.abstract: Tokenizers are a crucial component of latent diffusion models, as they define the latent space in which diffusion models operate. However, existing tokenizers are primarily designed to improve reconstruction fidelity or inherit pretrained representations, leaving unclear what kind of latent space is truly friendly for generative modeling. In this paper, we study this question from the perspective of latent manifold organization. By constructing controlled tokenizer variants, we identify three key properties of a diffusion-friendly latent manifold: coherent spatial structure, local manifold continuity, and global manifold semantics. We find that these properties are more consistent with downstream generation quality than reconstruction fidelity. Motivated by this finding, we propose the Prior-Aligned AutoEncoder (PAE), which explicitly shapes the latent manifold instead of leaving diffusion-friendly manifold to emerge indirectly from reconstruction or inheritance. Specifically, PAE leverages refined priors derived from VFMs and perturbation-based regularization to turn spatial structure, local continuity, and global semantics into explicit training objectives. On ImageNet 256x256, PAE improves both training efficiency and generation quality over existing tokenizers, reaching performance comparable to RAE with up to 13x faster convergence under the same training setup and achieving a new state-of-the-art gFID of 1.03. These results highlight the importance of organizing the latent manifold for latent diffusion models.
affiliations: Shenzhen Institutes of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences
2026-W20.filter_reason: The paper provides novel theoretical framing of latent space properties for diffusion models and proposes Prior-Aligned AutoEncoders with new training objectives (VFM-derived priors + perturbation-based regularization) that empirically outperform existing tokenizers with significant efficiency gains.
2026-W20.rating_rationale: Strong paper on latent diffusion tokenizers with solid institutional backing and clear novelty in latent manifold analysis, directly addressing video generation infrastructure; achieves SOTA results but lacks tier-1 industry pedigree and code/demo release, yet high topic relevance to world models/video gen frontier research elevates overall impact.
2026-W20.description: Introduces Prior-Aligned AutoEncoder (PAE), which explicitly shapes the latent manifold of tokenizers to be more suitable for latent diffusion models by enforcing coherent spatial structure, local continuity, and global semantics.
2026-W20.contribution: This work identifies three specific properties—coherent spatial structure, local manifold continuity, and global manifold semantics—that make a latent space friendly for diffusion-based generative modeling, independent of reconstruction fidelity. Unlike prior tokenizers that optimize for reconstruction or inherit pretrained representations, PAE directly engineers these properties using refined priors from Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) and perturbation-based regularization as explicit training objectives. This explicit manifold shaping leads to significantly faster convergence and improved generation quality compared to existing approaches.
2026-W20.limitations: The approach relies on priors derived from Vision Foundation Models (VFMs), which may introduce biases or limitations tied to the quality and scope of those pretrained models. The study primarily focuses on image generation on ImageNet 256x256, leaving generalization to other modalities or resolutions less established.

2026-W19.title: Generative Modeling with Flux Matching
2026-W19.abstract: We introduce Flux Matching, a new paradigm for generative modeling that generalizes existing score-based models to a broader family of vector fields that need not be conservative. Rather than requiring the model to equal the data score, the Flux Matching objective imposes a weaker condition that admits infinitely many vector fields whose stationary distribution is the data. This flexibility enables a class of generative models that cannot be learned under score matching, in which inductive biases, structural priors, and properties of the dynamics can be directly imposed or optimized. We show that Flux Matching performs strongly on high-dimensional image datasets and, more importantly, that our added freedom unlocks a range of applications including faster sampling, interpretable and mechanistic models, and dynamics that encode directed dependencies between variables. More broadly, Flux Matching opens a new dimension in generative modeling by turning the vector field itself into a design choice rather than a fixed target. Code is available at https://github.com/peterpaohuang/flux_matching.
affiliations: Department of Computer Science, Stanford University
2026-W19.filter_reason: Flux Matching proposes a fundamentally new generative modeling paradigm that generalizes score-based models by relaxing the conservative vector field constraint, enabling new capabilities (faster sampling, interpretable dynamics, directed dependencies) that cannot be achieved under existing score matching frameworks.
2026-W19.rating_rationale: Flux Matching is a genuine paradigm shift in generative modeling that generalizes score-based models and unlocks qualitatively new capabilities (faster sampling, mechanistic dynamics, directed dependencies), from active Stanford researchers with code released; strong relevance to video generation / world models via foundational generative modeling advances, though not directly targeting those domains.
2026-W19.description: Introduces Flux Matching, a generative modeling paradigm that learns non-conservative vector fields whose stationary distribution matches the data distribution.
2026-W19.contribution: Unlike score matching, which constrains the learned vector field to equal the data score (a conservative field), Flux Matching imposes only a weaker divergence-based condition that admits infinitely many valid vector fields. This relaxation transforms the vector field from a fixed target into a design choice, enabling direct imposition of inductive biases, structural priors, and dynamic properties. The framework subsumes score-based models as a special case while unlocking strictly new model classes that score matching cannot learn.
2026-W19.limitations: The paper does not deeply characterize which specific vector fields are theoretically optimal among the infinite valid solutions, leaving the design space underspecified. The added flexibility may require careful problem-specific design choices to realize its benefits in practice.

2026-W19.title: Learning Visual Feature-Based World Models via Residual Latent Action
2026-W19.abstract: World models predict future transitions from observations and actions. Existing works predominantly focus on image generation only. Visual feature-based world models, on the other hand, predict future visual features instead of raw video pixels, offering a promising alternative that is more efficient and less prone to hallucination. However, current feature-based approaches rely on direct regression, which leads to blurry or collapsed predictions in complex interactions, while generative modeling in high-dimensional feature spaces still remains challenging. In this work, we discover that a new type of latent action representation, which we refer to as *Residual Latent Action* (RLA), can be easily learned from DINO residuals. We also show that RLA is predictive, generalizable, and encodes temporal progression. Building on RLA, we propose *RLA World Model* (RLA-WM), which predicts RLA values via flow matching. RLA-WM outperforms both state-of-the-art feature-based and video-diffusion world models on simulation and real-world datasets, while being orders of magnitude faster than video diffusion. Furthermore, we develop two robot learning techniques that use RLA-WM to improve policy learning. The first one is a minimalist world action model with RLA that learns from actionless demonstration videos. The second one is the first visual RL framework trained entirely inside a world model learned from offline videos only, using a video-aligned reward and no online interactions or handcrafted rewards. Project page: https://mlzxy.github.io/rla-wm
2026-W19.filter_reason: Proposes a novel latent action representation (RLA) derived from DINO residuals that enables efficient visual feature-based world models via flow matching, bridging visual representation learning with world modeling while demonstrating new capabilities like learning from actionless videos and fully offline visual RL.
2026-W19.rating_rationale: Strong world model paper with genuine novelty (RLA from DINO residuals + flow matching for efficient feature-based prediction) and direct relevance to primary research interest, but from unknown authors at non-tier-1 institutions; likely 2-3 stars depending on execution quality and reproducibility.
2026-W19.description: Introduces a visual feature-based world model that predicts future DINO feature residuals using flow matching to enable efficient and accurate future state prediction.
2026-W19.contribution: The key novelty is the discovery of Residual Latent Action (RLA), a latent action representation learned from DINO feature residuals that is predictive, generalizable, and encodes temporal progression without requiring explicit action labels. Building on RLA, the proposed RLA-WM uses flow matching in feature space rather than pixel space, avoiding the blurry predictions of direct regression and the computational cost of video diffusion. The work also introduces the first visual RL framework trained entirely inside a world model learned from offline videos, using a video-aligned reward with no online interactions or handcrafted rewards.
2026-W19.limitations: The approach depends on DINO features as the visual representation, which may limit applicability to domains where pretrained DINO features are less informative. The method also assumes that future state information can be sufficiently captured in feature space, which may not hold for tasks requiring fine-grained pixel-level details.

2026-W19.title: GazeVLM: Active Vision via Internal Attention Control for Multimodal Reasoning
2026-W19.abstract: Human visual reasoning is governed by active vision, a process where metacognitive control drives top-down goal-directed attention, dynamically routing foveal focus toward task-relevant details while maintaining peripheral awareness of the global scene. In contrast, modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) process visual information passively, relying on the static accumulation of massive token contexts that dilute spatial reasoning and induce linguistic hallucinations. Here we propose the following paradigm shift: GazeVLM, a multimodal architecture that internalizes this metacognitive oversight over its deployment of attention resources directly into the reasoning loop. By empowering the VLM to autonomously generate gaze tokens ($\texttt{<LOOK>}$), GazeVLM establishes a top-down control mechanism over its own causal attention mask. The model dynamically dictates its focal intent, triggering a continuous suppression bias that dampens irrelevant visual features, implementing spatial selective attention and simulating foveal fixation. Once local reasoning concludes, the bias lifts, seamlessly restoring the global view. This architecture enables the model to fluidly transition between global spatial awareness and localized focal reasoning without relying on external agentic contraptions like cropping tools, or inflating the context window with additional visual tokens derived from localized visual patches. Trained with a bespoke Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) procedure that rewards valid grounding, our 4B-parameter GazeVLM delivers strong high-resolution multimodal reasoning performance, surpassing state-of-the-art VLMs in its parameter class by nearly 4% and agentic multimodal pipelines built around thinking with images by more than 5% on HRBench-4k and HRBench-8k.
2026-W19.filter_reason: Proposes a novel architectural mechanism (gaze tokens with dynamic attention control) that internalizes metacognitive attention routing directly into VLM reasoning, combined with a custom training objective (GRPO), demonstrating a qualitatively new capability for active vision in multimodal models without external tools.
2026-W19.rating_rationale: Active vision mechanism with internal attention control is a meaningful contribution to multimodal reasoning (novelty=2, not paradigm-shift), from active researchers at ETH (pedigree=2, not tier-1 industry), with moderate breadth in vision-language understanding and direct relevance to your primary interest in multimodal systems. Score: 0.30×0.5 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×1.0 = 0.60 → 2 stars ★
2026-W19.description: Introduces a multimodal architecture that enables Vision-Language Models to dynamically control their own attention through internally generated gaze tokens that suppress irrelevant visual features and simulate foveal fixation.
2026-W19.contribution: GazeVLM internalizes metacognitive attention control directly into the reasoning loop by allowing the model to autonomously generate special gaze tokens that modulate its causal attention mask, implementing spatial selective attention without external tools or additional visual tokens. Unlike prior VLMs that passively accumulate static token contexts, this top-down control mechanism enables fluid transitions between global spatial awareness and localized focal reasoning entirely within the model's architecture. The model is trained with a bespoke GRPO procedure that rewards valid grounding, making the gaze-driven attention control learnable end-to-end.
2026-W19.limitations: The approach requires training with a specialized GRPO reward procedure, which may limit generalizability and increase training complexity. The design assumes that suppressing irrelevant visual features via attention bias is sufficient for high-resolution reasoning, which may not capture all forms of complex spatial dependencies.

2026-W19.title: Tracing the Arrow of Time: Diagnosing Temporal Information Flow in Video-LLMs
2026-W19.abstract: The Arrow-of-Time (AoT) task, determining whether a video plays forward or backward by recognizing temporal irreversibility, is one humans solve with near-perfect accuracy, yet frontier Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) perform only modestly above chance. This gap raises a key question: do visual backbones fail to encode temporal information, or does information bottleneck lie elsewhere in the Video-LLM architecture? We address this question by isolating the vision encoder from the Video-LLM and tracing temporal information across the encoder, projector, and LLM. We find that video-centric encoders with explicit temporal modeling encode strong temporal signals, whereas frame-centric encoders do not. However, when video-centric representations are passed through a standard Video-LLM architecture, performance often collapses, revealing a bottleneck of temporal information flow. We identify projector design as a key factor: Q-Former disrupts temporal information, while a time-preserved MLP projection substantially improves the LLM's access to such information. Our layer-wise analysis further shows temporal representation dynamics across encoder layers. Guided by these findings, we build a Video-LLM with temporal-aware video-centric encoder, time-preserved projector, and AoT supervision, surpassing human performance on AoT$_{PPB}$ with 98.1\% accuracy, and improving broader temporal reasoning tasks by up to 6.0 points on VITATECS-Direction and 1.3 points on TVBench. Our results show that temporal reasoning in Video-LLMs requires both effective temporal encoding and reliable transfer of this information to the LLM.
affiliations: Center for Information and Neural Networks
National Institute of Information and Communications Technology
2026-W19.filter_reason: The paper provides novel architectural insights by systematically diagnosing temporal information bottlenecks in Video-LLMs, identifies projector design (Q-Former vs MLP) as a critical failure point, and demonstrates that temporal reasoning requires both effective encoding AND reliable information transfer—yielding actionable design principles for the Video-LLM community.
2026-W19.rating_rationale: Strong fit for video generation/temporal reasoning interest; systematic diagnostic approach (novelty=2, passes filter) with actionable architectural insights; university-affiliated authors without tier-1 industry lab pedigree; temporal bottleneck findings advance Video-LLM design but don't fundamentally shift the field; no code/demo mentioned; published analysis of existing architectures (Q-Former vs MLP) is well-timed but not first-mover; directly targets primary research interest (video generation + world models require temporal understanding). Total = 0.30×0.5 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×1.0 = 0.55 → 2 stars.
2026-W19.description: Diagnoses where temporal information is lost in Video-LLM architectures by tracing temporal signals across the encoder, projector, and LLM components.
2026-W19.contribution: The paper identifies that temporal information bottlenecks in Video-LLMs arise not only from weak visual encoding but critically from projector design — specifically showing that Q-Former disrupts temporal information while a time-preserved MLP projection maintains it. It further reveals that video-centric encoders with explicit temporal modeling encode strong temporal signals, but these are lost when passed through standard Video-LLM architectures. By combining a temporal-aware encoder, time-preserved projector, and Arrow-of-Time supervision, the work demonstrates that both encoding and reliable transfer are necessary for temporal reasoning.
2026-W19.limitations: The analysis focuses primarily on the Arrow-of-Time task as a diagnostic probe for temporal reasoning, which may not capture the full spectrum of temporal understanding required in real-world video comprehension. The findings are also contingent on the specific architectures examined, and generalization to other projector or encoder designs is not fully established.

2026-W19.title: Diffusion-APO: Trajectory-Aware Direct Preference Alignment for Video Diffusion Transformers
2026-W19.abstract: Efficiently aligning large-scale video diffusion models with human intent requires a scalable and trajectory-aware pathway that bridges the inherent discrepancy between training noise distributions and practical inference trajectories. While existing paradigms such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) attempt to address this, they are often hindered by either reliance on bias-prone, complex reward models or suboptimal timestep sampling. In this paper, we propose Diffusion-APO (Aligned Preference Optimization), a trajectory-aware algorithm that resolves this misalignment by synchronizing training noise with inference-time denoising paths to maximize gradient signal efficacy. To translate this algorithmic innovation into a practical solution, we introduce a unified and modular RLHF framework that integrates online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction. This framework enables flexible, multi-stage preference alignment across diverse data and computational constraints without relying on scalar-reward-based policy gradients. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that Diffusion-APO consistently outperforms standard baselines in visual quality and instruction following, while effectively preserving generative fidelity during model acceleration, providing a robust, end-to-end pathway for scalable video diffusion alignment.
2026-W19.filter_reason: Proposes a novel trajectory-aware alignment algorithm (Diffusion-APO) that addresses the fundamental mismatch between training noise distributions and inference trajectories in video diffusion models, coupled with a practical unified RLHF framework that goes beyond applying existing DPO/GRPO methods.
2026-W19.rating_rationale: Solid video diffusion alignment work addressing a real training-inference mismatch problem with a practical framework; primary affiliation appears to be Chinese institution (not tier-1 listed lab), passes novelty filter but not paradigm-shifting, highly relevant to primary interest (video generation) but no code/demo release mentioned. Total = 0.30×0.5 + 0.20×0.5 + 0.15×0.5 + 0.10×0.5 + 0.05×0 + 0.05×0.5 + 0.15×1.0 = 0.55 → 2 stars.
2026-W19.description: Introduces a trajectory-aware preference alignment algorithm for video diffusion transformers that synchronizes training noise distributions with inference-time denoising paths to improve gradient signal efficacy.
2026-W19.contribution: Diffusion-APO addresses the fundamental mismatch between training noise distributions and practical inference trajectories in video diffusion model alignment, which prior DPO and GRPO methods fail to resolve. It introduces a unified modular RLHF framework combining online ranking, half-online anchoring, offline refinement, and distillation-aware drift correction, enabling multi-stage preference alignment without relying on scalar-reward-based policy gradients. This trajectory-aware formulation provides a scalable, end-to-end pathway that also preserves generative fidelity during model acceleration.
2026-W19.limitations: The approach assumes that synchronizing training noise with inference trajectories is sufficient to bridge the train-inference gap, which may not hold for all sampling schedules or model architectures. The multi-stage framework introduces additional complexity and may require careful tuning of its modular components across different data and computational regimes.
